metadata.name: claude-cowork-dojo
metadata.description: Interactive learning path for mastering Claude Cowork -- the autonomous desktop agent for knowledge workers. Covers setup, delegation, file management, skills, connectors, plugins, scheduled tasks, projects, sub-agents, computer use, dispatch, advanced skill engineering, power user workflows, and optimization.
sourceFiles: Anthropic Official Skill Building Guide (32 pages)

modules.title: What is Cowork (and When to Use It)
modules.description: Understand the three Claude surfaces -- Chat, Cowork, and Code -- and learn when Cowork is the right tool for the job.
modules.prerequisites: A Claude Pro ($20/mo), Max, or Team subscription. Claude Desktop app installed.

concepts.title: Three Surfaces, Three Jobs
concepts.body: Chat is for questions and conversation -- you get answers in text. Cowork is for workflows and deliverables -- you get files, reports, and automated outputs. Code is for software development -- you get commits, PRs, and tested code. Cowork sits in the middle: it has file access, sub-agents, and tool use, but it is designed for knowledge workers, not developers.

concepts.title: How Cowork Differs From Chat
concepts.body: Cowork can read and write files on your machine, spawn sub-agents to work in parallel, use connectors (Gmail, Calendar, Slack, Notion), run skills (reusable instruction sets), and produce tangible deliverables (spreadsheets, PDFs, presentations). Chat can only produce text responses. If your task ends with a file or an action, Cowork is the right surface.

concepts.title: The VM Sandbox Model
concepts.body: Cowork runs inside a sandboxed virtual machine on your desktop. It can access files in your designated work folder but cannot reach system files, install software, or run arbitrary code outside the sandbox. This is a safety feature: Cowork is powerful but contained. Your files stay local -- nothing is uploaded to the cloud unless you explicitly use a connector.

prompts.label: Try Your First Cowork Task
prompts.prompt:
Create a file called "hello-cowork.md" in my work folder. Include:
- Today's date
- A one-paragraph summary of what Cowork can do
- A bulleted list of 5 tasks I could delegate to Cowork this week
- A section called "Next Steps" with links to learn more
prompts.expectedOutcome: A markdown file is created in your work folder, demonstrating that Cowork can create and write files -- something Chat cannot do.

prompts.label: Compare Chat vs Cowork Output
prompts.prompt:
I want to understand the difference between Chat and Cowork. For this task:
1. Explain in 3 sentences what Chat would produce if I asked it to "create a weekly meal plan"
2. Now actually create the meal plan as a formatted markdown file called "meal-plan.md" with 7 days, breakfast/lunch/dinner, and a grocery list at the bottom
3. At the end, note which parts only Cowork could do (the file creation, the formatting, the deliverable)
prompts.expectedOutcome: A tangible meal plan file plus a clear explanation of why Cowork was needed -- demonstrating the deliverable vs conversation distinction.

prompts.label: Explore the Cowork Interface
prompts.prompt:
Give me a quick tour of the Cowork interface. Explain:
1. Where do I type prompts?
2. How do I see what files Cowork has created or modified?
3. How do I access Settings to configure Cowork?
4. How do I switch between Chat and Cowork mode?
5. What does the activity feed show me?
6. How do I stop a task that is running too long?
prompts.expectedOutcome: A concise orientation guide to the Cowork interface, helping new users navigate confidently.

prompts.label: Plan Before You Act With Claude
prompts.prompt:
Experience Claude Code's 'describe the end state, not the steps' principle in action:

1. Create a folder called `cowork-demo` with 3-4 messy, unorganized text files inside (mix of topics like recipes, todo items, and random notes — all in one folder with no structure).
2. Ask Claude to look at the folder and describe what it sees, then ask it to propose a plan for organizing the files into a logical structure — but NOT to make any changes yet.
3. Read Claude's plan carefully. Ask it to explain its reasoning for any organizational decision you find surprising.
4. Tell Claude your desired end state in one sentence (e.g., 'I want each file in a subfolder matching its topic, with a README listing what's inside'). Ask Claude to revise its plan to match that outcome.
5. Approve the revised plan and let Claude execute it. Verify the final folder structure matches what you described.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A reorganized folder whose structure matches the end-state description you gave Claude, produced through a plan-first workflow rather than step-by-step instructions.

prompts.label: Set Up Your First Cowork Project
prompts.prompt:
Explore Claude Code as an AI agent that can take actions on your computer — not just chat. Follow these steps:

1. Ask Claude to explain the difference between a traditional AI chat (like ChatGPT) and an AI agent like Claude Code, in 3 bullet points.
2. Ask Claude to create a folder called `cowork-intro` and inside it create a file called `about-me.txt` with a few placeholder lines describing a fictional person's name, role, and top 3 goals.
3. Ask Claude to read the file back and then rewrite it, making the goals more specific and actionable.
4. Ask Claude to create a second file called `daily-tasks.md` inside `cowork-intro` that lists 5 realistic daily tasks the fictional person could delegate to an AI agent.
5. Ask Claude to summarize what it just did across all steps in one short paragraph, as if briefing a new team member.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A `cowork-intro` folder containing an updated `about-me.txt` and a `daily-tasks.md` file, giving you hands-on experience with Claude Code acting as an agent that reads, writes, and iterates on files autonomously.

prompts.label: Build a Reusable Claude Skill
prompts.prompt:
In this exercise, you will explore Claude's 'Skills' concept by building a reusable prompt template inside Claude Code that automates a recurring task.

1. Ask Claude to explain what a 'Skill' is in the context of Claude's ecosystem — a reusable, saved prompt that automates a recurring task without coding.
2. Ask Claude to help you design a reusable Skill for a specific use case, such as: 'Summarize a weekly team standup update into 3 bullet points with action items.'
3. Ask Claude to write the full system prompt for that Skill, including placeholders like {{standup_notes}} so it can be reused with different inputs.
4. Test the Skill by asking Claude to run it with a sample input you provide (e.g., paste in a fake standup update).
5. Ask Claude to suggest two more Skill ideas suited for marketing, finance, or product teams, and pick one to draft together.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A reusable Skill prompt template with clear placeholders, tested against a sample input and ready to be saved for repeated use.

modules.proTip: If your task ends with a question mark, use Chat. If it ends with a file, a report, or an action taken on your behalf, use Cowork.
takeaways: Chat answers questions, Cowork produces deliverables, Code writes software
Cowork has file access, sub-agents, connectors, and skills that Chat does not
The VM sandbox keeps your system safe while giving Cowork controlled file access
Default to Cowork whenever the output is a file, spreadsheet, email, or automated workflow

modules.title: Setup and Configuration
modules.description: Configure your Cowork environment with a dedicated work folder, global instructions, and context files that make Claude understand your world.
modules.prerequisites: Module 1 completed. Claude Desktop app installed with Pro ($20/mo), Max, or Team plan.

concepts.title: The Dedicated Work Folder
concepts.body: Never point Cowork at your Documents or Desktop folder. Create a dedicated folder (e.g., ~/CoworkSpace or C:\CoworkSpace) with a clear structure. This becomes Cowork's workspace -- everything it reads and writes lives here. A clean folder structure is the single biggest predictor of Cowork productivity.

concepts.title: Global Instructions
concepts.body: Go to Settings > Cowork > Edit Global Instructions. This is text that Cowork reads at the start of every session. Put your name, role, company, communication preferences, and recurring context here. Think of it as your permanent briefing to an executive assistant. Example: 'I am Sarah, VP of Marketing at Acme Corp. I prefer bullet points over paragraphs. Always save files in markdown format.'

concepts.title: Context Files: CLAUDE.md and Friends
concepts.body: Drop these files in your work folder root: CLAUDE.md (project-specific instructions), branding.md (logos, colors, voice guidelines), memories.md (things Cowork should remember across sessions). Cowork reads these automatically. They are your persistent memory system -- the more specific, the better the output.

concepts.title: The Scaffold: Folder Structure
concepts.body: A well-organized folder structure tells Cowork where to find and put things. Recommended: thoughts/ (brainstorming, drafts), ideas/ (concepts in progress), todo/ (active tasks), outputs/ (finished deliverables), done/ (archived completed work), references/ (source materials, brand guides, templates). Cowork navigates this structure and files outputs in the right place.

prompts.label: Set Up Global Instructions
prompts.prompt:
Help me write my Global Instructions for Cowork. Ask me 5 questions about my role, preferences, and work style, then generate a complete Global Instructions block I can paste into Settings > Cowork > Edit. Include:
- My name and role
- My company and industry
- Communication style preferences
- Default file formats
- Any recurring context (tools I use, people I work with, projects I manage)
prompts.expectedOutcome: A ready-to-paste Global Instructions block tailored to your specific role and preferences.

prompts.label: Scaffold a Project Folder
prompts.prompt:
Create a complete Cowork project folder structure in my work directory. Include:
1. A CLAUDE.md file with project-specific instructions (use a marketing campaign as the example project)
2. A branding.md with placeholder sections for logo, colors, voice, and audience
3. A memories.md with sections for people, decisions, and lessons learned
4. Empty folders: thoughts/, ideas/, todo/, outputs/, done/, references/
5. A README.md explaining what each folder is for

Make each file have real, useful starter content -- not just headers.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A fully scaffolded project folder with populated context files, ready for productive Cowork sessions.

prompts.label: Configure Context Files for Maximum Productivity
prompts.prompt:
I want to optimize my Cowork context for maximum productivity. Review my current work folder and:
1. Check if CLAUDE.md exists -- if not, create one with best practices
2. Check if branding.md exists -- if not, create one with my company details
3. Check if memories.md exists -- if not, create one with starter sections
4. Audit the folder structure -- suggest improvements
5. Create a checklist of the top 10 things that should be in my context files
6. Score my current setup out of 10 and explain how to reach a 9 or 10
prompts.expectedOutcome: A complete context audit with created or improved files and a scored rubric for measuring setup quality.

modules.proTip: The quality of Cowork's output is directly proportional to the quality of your context files. Spend 30 minutes setting up CLAUDE.md properly and you will save hours every week.
takeaways: Always use a dedicated work folder -- never point Cowork at Documents or Desktop
Global Instructions persist across all sessions and set your permanent context
CLAUDE.md, branding.md, and memories.md are your persistent memory system
A clear folder structure (thoughts, ideas, todo, outputs, done, references) guides Cowork's file organization

modules.title: The Delegation Framework
modules.description: Master the art of delegating to Cowork with end-state prompting, the three-question framework, and change log discipline.
modules.prerequisites: Module 2 completed. Work folder set up with context files.

concepts.title: The Three-Question Framework
concepts.body: Before every Cowork task, answer three questions: (1) What does 'done' look like? Describe the exact output -- file name, format, sections, length. (2) What context does Claude need? Point to reference files, examples, brand guides. (3) What constraints can it not guess? Deadlines, audience, tone, things to avoid. These three answers are your prompt.

concepts.title: End-State Prompting
concepts.body: Vague instructions produce vague results. Instead of 'write a report about our Q1 results,' say 'Create a file called q1-report.md with: (1) Executive summary (3 sentences), (2) Revenue table with Q1 vs Q4 comparison, (3) Top 3 wins, (4) Top 3 risks, (5) Recommended actions. Use data from references/q1-data.csv. Tone: professional, concise. Length: under 500 words.' The more specific your end-state, the better the output.

concepts.title: Always Ask for a Change Log
concepts.body: Add 'Include a change log at the end listing every file you created, modified, or deleted' to your prompts. This gives you an audit trail of exactly what Cowork did. Without it, you are trusting blindly. The change log is your receipt -- review it every time.

concepts.title: Delegation Tips: Dummy Files and Source Tracing
concepts.body: Create dummy files (empty templates with headers) before delegating. This shows Cowork the exact structure you want. Source tracing: ask Cowork to cite which reference files it used for each section. Token tracking: ask Cowork to estimate how many tokens the task consumed so you can optimize future prompts.

prompts.label: Delegate a File Organization Task
prompts.prompt:
Organize my Downloads folder. Here is what 'done' looks like:
1. Scan all files in ~/Downloads (or C:\Users\[me]\Downloads)
2. Create subfolders by type: Documents/, Images/, Spreadsheets/, PDFs/, Archives/, Other/
3. Move each file into the appropriate subfolder based on extension
4. Create a manifest.md listing every file moved, its original name, new location, and file size
5. Do NOT delete anything -- only move files into subfolders

Constraints: Skip any file larger than 500MB. Skip any file modified in the last 24 hours. Include a change log.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A clean Downloads folder with files sorted into typed subfolders, plus a manifest documenting every move.

prompts.label: Write an End-State Prompt
prompts.prompt:
I need to write a client proposal but I am not sure how to prompt Cowork effectively. Help me build the prompt using the three-question framework:

1. Ask me what 'done' looks like (deliverable format, sections, length)
2. Ask me what context Claude needs (client name, project scope, pricing)
3. Ask me what constraints it cannot guess (tone, deadline, things to avoid)

Then combine my answers into a single, optimized end-state prompt I can paste into Cowork. Format it as a ready-to-use delegation prompt.
prompts.expectedOutcome: An interactive prompt-building session that produces a polished, copy-paste ready delegation prompt.

prompts.label: Create a Delegation Template Library
prompts.prompt:
Create a file called delegation-templates.md with 10 reusable delegation templates for common knowledge work tasks. Each template should follow the three-question framework and include:
- Task name
- The 'done' state (exact deliverable description)
- Required context (what files or info to provide)
- Default constraints
- A ready-to-use prompt with [PLACEHOLDERS] for customization

Include templates for: meeting notes, client proposal, expense report, weekly status update, competitive analysis, email draft, presentation outline, project brief, hiring scorecard, and content calendar.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A reusable template library with 10 delegation prompts following the three-question framework, ready for daily use.

modules.proTip: The biggest mistake new Cowork users make is prompting like they are chatting. Cowork is an executor, not a conversationalist. Give it a spec, not a conversation starter.
takeaways: Answer three questions before every task: What is done? What context is needed? What constraints exist?
End-state prompting with specific deliverable descriptions beats vague instructions every time
Always request a change log to audit what Cowork created, modified, or deleted
Build a template library for recurring tasks -- reuse beats reinvention

modules.title: File and Document Management
modules.description: Use Cowork to sort, rename, batch process, and create professional documents -- Excel, PowerPoint, PDF, invoices, and more.
modules.prerequisites: Module 3 completed. Files in your work folder to practice with.

concepts.title: File Sorting and Batch Operations
concepts.body: Cowork excels at file operations that would take you hours manually. Sorting hundreds of files by type, date, or content. Renaming files with consistent naming conventions. Extracting data from multiple files and consolidating it. Moving files between folders based on rules. The key is being specific about the rules -- Cowork follows instructions literally.

concepts.title: Document Creation: Beyond Text
concepts.body: Cowork can create Excel spreadsheets with formulas, charts, and formatting. PowerPoint presentations with layouts, bullet points, and speaker notes. Formatted PDFs with headers, tables, and page numbers. The built-in skills (xlsx, pptx, pdf, docx) handle the heavy lifting -- you describe what you want, the skill handles the format.

concepts.title: Receipt Processing and Expense Tracking
concepts.body: Cowork can read receipts (images or PDFs), extract vendor, date, amount, and category, then populate an expense spreadsheet. This is a classic automation: scan a folder of receipt photos, extract structured data from each, output a consolidated expense report. Pair it with a scheduled task for weekly processing.

prompts.label: Organize and Rename Files in Bulk
prompts.prompt:
I have a messy folder of client documents. Organize them:
1. Scan all files in my references/ folder
2. Rename each file to follow this convention: YYYY-MM-DD_ClientName_DocType.ext (extract dates from file content or modified date)
3. Create subfolders by client name
4. Move renamed files into the correct client subfolder
5. Generate a file-inventory.md with columns: Original Name, New Name, Client, Type, Date, Size

Constraints: Do not modify file contents -- only rename and move. Include a change log.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A clean, consistently named file structure organized by client, with a full inventory document.

prompts.label: Generate a Professional Invoice
prompts.prompt:
Create a professional invoice as a PDF. Details:
- From: [My Company Name], [Address], [Email]
- To: Acme Corp, 123 Business Ave, New York, NY 10001
- Invoice #: INV-2026-042
- Date: Today's date, Due: Net 30
- Line items:
  1. Strategy Consulting -- 20 hours @ $150/hr = $3,000
  2. Market Research Report -- 1 deliverable @ $2,500 = $2,500
  3. Presentation Design -- 8 hours @ $100/hr = $800
- Subtotal, Tax (8%), and Total
- Payment terms and bank details section (placeholder)

Save as outputs/invoice-acme-2026-04.pdf
prompts.expectedOutcome: A formatted PDF invoice with proper line items, calculations, tax, and professional layout.

prompts.label: Create a Budget Spreadsheet with Formulas
prompts.prompt:
Create an Excel spreadsheet called outputs/q2-budget.xlsx with:
1. Sheet 1 "Monthly Budget": Columns for Category, April, May, June, Q2 Total. Rows for: Payroll, Marketing, Software, Travel, Office, Misc. Add SUM formulas for each month column and Q2 Total. Include sample data.
2. Sheet 2 "Actuals vs Budget": Same categories with Budget, Actual, and Variance columns. Conditional formatting: green if under budget, red if over.
3. Sheet 3 "Summary Dashboard": A summary table pulling totals from both sheets. Include percentage variance calculations.

Format with professional styling -- bold headers, currency formatting, borders.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A multi-sheet Excel workbook with formulas, conditional formatting, and professional styling ready for use.

prompts.label: Automate Document Reformatting Pipeline
prompts.prompt:
Build a hands-on document automation workflow inside Claude Code that mimics real-world file management tasks:

1. Ask Claude to create a sample folder structure with three subfolders: `inbox/`, `processed/`, and `archive/`, then populate `inbox/` with three mock invoice text files (invoice_001.txt, invoice_002.txt, invoice_003.txt), each containing realistic fields like vendor name, amount, date, and status.
2. Ask Claude to write a Python script called `reformat_invoices.py` that reads each file in `inbox/`, extracts the key fields, reformats them into a standardized Markdown summary, saves the output to `processed/`, and moves the original file to `archive/`.
3. Run the script and ask Claude to verify the output by displaying the contents of one processed Markdown file and confirming all originals have moved to `archive/`.
4. Ask Claude to extend the script to generate a `weekly_briefing.md` file in the root folder that summarizes all processed invoices — listing total count, combined amount, and any flagged as unpaid.
5. Ask Claude to add a simple log file (`run_log.txt`) that timestamps each script run and records how many files were processed, so you have an audit trail.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A working Python automation pipeline that reformats invoice files into structured Markdown summaries, archives originals, generates a weekly briefing report, and maintains a timestamped run log.

modules.proTip: Cowork's built-in skills (xlsx, pptx, pdf, docx) are purpose-built for document creation. Invoke them by name or let Cowork auto-detect the right skill from your file extension.
takeaways: Cowork handles file sorting, renaming, and batch operations that would take hours manually
Built-in skills create professional Excel, PowerPoint, PDF, and Word documents
Always specify exact file names, formats, and output locations in your prompts
Pair receipt processing with scheduled tasks for automated weekly expense tracking

modules.title: Skills System Mastery
modules.description: Understand the skills system -- built-in skills, custom SKILL.md files, progressive disclosure, and skill stacking.
modules.prerequisites: Module 4 completed. Familiarity with markdown files.

concepts.title: What is a Skill?
concepts.body: A skill is a SKILL.md file -- a set of reusable instructions that Cowork loads when triggered. It has YAML frontmatter (name, description, trigger conditions) and a body with step-by-step instructions. Think of it as a playbook: instead of re-explaining a complex workflow every time, you write it once as a skill and invoke it with a slash command.

concepts.title: Built-in Skills
concepts.body: Cowork ships with built-in skills for common tasks: pdf (read, merge, split PDFs), docx (create and edit Word documents), pptx (create presentations), xlsx (create spreadsheets with formulas), canvas-design (create visual designs). These are pre-installed and optimized -- always check if a built-in skill exists before building a custom one.

concepts.title: Progressive Disclosure
concepts.body: Skills use a two-stage loading system to save tokens. First, only the metadata loads (~100 tokens: name and description). The full instructions and linked files only load when the skill is actually triggered. This means having 20 skills installed costs only ~2,000 tokens in overhead, not 20,000. The entire skill system targets roughly 2% of the context window.

concepts.title: Skill Stacking
concepts.body: Multiple skills can be active in the same session. Cowork picks the right skill based on your prompt. You can also explicitly invoke skills by name. Stacking lets you build powerful combinations: a research skill feeds into a report-writing skill, which feeds into an email-drafting skill. Each skill is a composable building block.

prompts.label: Create Your First Custom Skill
prompts.prompt:
Create a custom Cowork skill that generates a weekly status update. The SKILL.md should:
1. Have proper YAML frontmatter with name, description, and trigger conditions
2. Accept inputs: project name, key accomplishments, blockers, next week's priorities
3. Output a formatted markdown file with sections: Summary, Accomplishments, Blockers, Next Week, Risks
4. Include a professional tone and bullet-point format
5. Save the output to outputs/status-update-[date].md

Explain each part of the SKILL.md structure as you build it.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A complete SKILL.md file with frontmatter, instructions, and output format -- plus an explanation of each component.

prompts.label: Test a Built-in Skill
prompts.prompt:
I want to test the built-in PDF skill. Walk me through:
1. How to invoke the PDF skill
2. Create a simple 2-page PDF with: a title page (company name, report title, date) and a content page (3 sections with headers and body text)
3. Save it as outputs/test-report.pdf
4. Then show me how to read an existing PDF and extract its text content
5. Finally, explain what other built-in skills are available and how to discover them
prompts.expectedOutcome: A working PDF created via the built-in skill, plus a guide to discovering and using all built-in skills.

prompts.label: Customize an Existing Skill
prompts.prompt:
Take the built-in morning briefing skill and customize it for my specific needs. I want a daily briefing that includes:
1. Weather for my city (using web search)
2. My top 3 calendar events today (using Calendar connector)
3. Unread email summary -- senders and subjects only (using Gmail connector)
4. Top 3 AI news headlines (using web search)
5. My active tasks from todo/ folder

Create a custom SKILL.md that wraps the built-in skill with my personalized sections. Save it so I can trigger it daily.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A customized morning briefing skill that combines multiple data sources into a personalized daily dashboard.

modules.proTip: Use the Skill Creator (available in Cowork settings) to build skills interactively. It walks you through frontmatter, triggers, instructions, and testing -- much faster than writing SKILL.md from scratch.
takeaways: Skills are reusable SKILL.md playbooks with YAML frontmatter and step-by-step instructions
Built-in skills (pdf, docx, pptx, xlsx) handle common document tasks out of the box
Progressive disclosure keeps token overhead low -- metadata loads first, full instructions only when triggered
Skill stacking lets you chain multiple skills in a single session for complex workflows

modules.title: Connectors and Integrations
modules.description: Connect Cowork to external services -- Gmail, Calendar, Slack, Notion, and 8000+ apps via Zapier MCP.
modules.prerequisites: Module 5 completed. At least one service account (Gmail, Calendar, etc.) to connect.

concepts.title: Built-in Connectors
concepts.body: Cowork has 50+ built-in connectors: Gmail, Google Calendar, Slack, Notion, HubSpot, Canva, Google Drive, and more. Each connector gives Cowork API-level access to that service -- reading emails, creating calendar events, posting to Slack channels, querying Notion databases. Configure them in Settings > Customize > Connectors.

concepts.title: Zapier MCP for Everything Else
concepts.body: For services without a built-in connector, Zapier MCP bridges the gap. It connects Cowork to 8,000+ apps through Zapier's automation platform. Set up a Zapier MCP server, authenticate your apps in Zapier, and Cowork can trigger any Zapier action. This is the escape hatch for niche tools.

concepts.title: Pairing Connectors with Skills
concepts.body: The real power comes from combining connectors with skills. A morning briefing skill that pulls from Gmail + Calendar + Slack. A client research skill that queries HubSpot + LinkedIn. An expense tracking skill that reads Gmail receipts and writes to Google Sheets. Connectors provide the data, skills provide the workflow.

prompts.label: Connect Gmail and Build an Email Summary
prompts.prompt:
I want to connect my Gmail to Cowork. Walk me through:
1. How to enable the Gmail connector in Settings
2. How to authenticate (what permissions are needed)
3. Once connected, search my inbox for unread emails from the last 24 hours
4. Create a summary file called outputs/email-summary.md with: sender, subject, date, and a 1-sentence preview for each email
5. Group emails by category: Action Required, FYI, Newsletters, Other

Do not read full email bodies -- only subjects and senders.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A configured Gmail connector plus an organized email summary file grouped by priority category.

prompts.label: Build a Morning Briefing with Multiple Connectors
prompts.prompt:
Create a comprehensive morning briefing using multiple connectors. Pull data from:
1. Gmail: Count of unread emails, top 5 by sender importance
2. Calendar: Today's meetings with times, attendees, and any prep notes
3. Web search: Top 3 industry news headlines relevant to my field

Combine everything into a single briefing file: outputs/morning-briefing-[date].md

Format it with clear sections, time estimates for each meeting, and action items extracted from email subjects. Keep it under 1 page -- this is a scan-and-go document.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A concise, multi-source morning briefing combining email, calendar, and news into a single actionable document.

prompts.label: Set Up Zapier MCP for a Niche Integration
prompts.prompt:
I want to connect Cowork to a tool that does not have a built-in connector (example: Airtable, Trello, or Asana). Walk me through:
1. How to set up Zapier MCP as a connector
2. How to authenticate Zapier with my target app
3. Create a test workflow: when I say 'sync tasks', pull my open tasks from the target app and save them as todo/external-tasks.md
4. Explain the limitations of Zapier MCP vs built-in connectors
5. List the top 10 most useful Zapier integrations for knowledge workers
prompts.expectedOutcome: A working Zapier MCP connection with a test workflow, plus guidance on when to use Zapier vs built-in connectors.

modules.proTip: Start with Gmail and Calendar connectors -- they cover 80% of knowledge worker workflows. Add Slack and Notion next. Only reach for Zapier MCP when there is no built-in option.
takeaways: 50+ built-in connectors give Cowork API access to Gmail, Calendar, Slack, Notion, and more
Zapier MCP extends reach to 8,000+ apps when built-in connectors are not available
Pair connectors with skills for powerful automated workflows
Configure connectors in Settings > Customize > Connectors

modules.title: Plugins and Plugin Building
modules.description: Install, customize, and build plugins -- bundles of skills, connectors, sub-agents, and slash commands packaged for distribution.
modules.prerequisites: Module 6 completed. Understanding of skills and connectors.

concepts.title: What Plugins Are
concepts.body: A plugin is a package that bundles multiple skills, connector configurations, sub-agent definitions, and slash commands into a single installable unit. Think of a skill as a single recipe and a plugin as an entire cookbook. Example: a 'Sales Operations' plugin might include skills for lead research, proposal generation, CRM updates, and pipeline reporting -- all preconfigured to work together.

concepts.title: The Plugin Marketplace
concepts.body: Anthropic launched the Plugin Marketplace in February 2026. It is a curated collection of community-built plugins. Browse by category (productivity, sales, marketing, finance, engineering), install with one click, and customize to your needs. Before building a plugin from scratch, check the marketplace -- someone may have already solved your problem.

concepts.title: Building Your Own Plugin
concepts.body: A plugin is a folder containing: a manifest.json (metadata, dependencies, permissions), one or more SKILL.md files, optional connector configurations, and optional sub-agent definitions. Build plugins for workflows you repeat across projects or want to share with your team. Start simple: one skill, one slash command. Expand as you validate the workflow.

prompts.label: Install and Explore a Plugin
prompts.prompt:
I want to explore the Cowork plugin ecosystem. Help me:
1. Explain how to access the Plugin Marketplace
2. Browse available plugins and recommend 3 that would be useful for a marketing professional
3. Walk me through installing one plugin
4. Show me how to list all installed plugins and their available commands
5. Test one command from the installed plugin and explain the output
prompts.expectedOutcome: A guided tour of the Plugin Marketplace with a working installed plugin and tested command.

prompts.label: Customize a Plugin for Your Workflow
prompts.prompt:
Take an installed plugin and customize it for my specific needs:
1. List the skills and commands included in the plugin
2. Identify which skill is closest to my use case but needs modification
3. Fork the skill -- create a customized version that:
   - Uses my company's branding (from branding.md)
   - Outputs files in my preferred format
   - Includes my specific workflow steps
4. Replace the original skill with my customized version
5. Test the customized version and compare output quality
prompts.expectedOutcome: A customized plugin skill tailored to your specific workflow, tested and validated against the original.

prompts.label: Build a Simple Plugin from Scratch
prompts.prompt:
Build a 'Client Management' plugin from scratch. The plugin should include:
1. A manifest.json with name, description, version, and dependencies
2. Skill 1: client-research.md -- research a company using web search and produce a brief
3. Skill 2: meeting-prep.md -- prepare for a client meeting using calendar + email data
4. Skill 3: follow-up.md -- draft a follow-up email after a meeting
5. A slash command /client that accepts a company name and runs the research skill

Save everything in a plugin/ folder with proper structure. Include a README explaining how to install and use it.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A complete plugin folder with manifest, 3 skills, a slash command, and documentation -- ready for installation or sharing.

modules.proTip: Start by customizing existing plugins before building from scratch. Most knowledge worker needs are covered by marketplace plugins with minor tweaks.
takeaways: Plugins bundle skills, connectors, sub-agents, and commands into installable packages
Check the Plugin Marketplace before building from scratch -- save time by customizing existing plugins
Start simple: one skill, one command. Expand as you validate the workflow
Plugin structure: manifest.json + SKILL.md files + optional connector configs + sub-agent definitions

modules.title: Scheduled Tasks
modules.description: Automate recurring work with scheduled tasks -- daily briefings, weekly reports, expense tracking, and more.
modules.prerequisites: Module 6 completed. At least one connector configured (Gmail or Calendar).

concepts.title: Creating Scheduled Tasks
concepts.body: Use the /schedule command to create recurring tasks. Specify a schedule (daily, weekly, monthly, or custom cron), a prompt (what Cowork should do), and optionally a skill to invoke. Scheduled tasks run automatically at the specified time. They are the foundation of a 'set it and forget it' automation system.

concepts.title: Scheduling Patterns
concepts.body: Common patterns: daily morning briefing (8am, pulls email + calendar + news), weekly status report (Friday 4pm, summarizes the week's output), monthly expense processing (1st of month, scans receipts and generates report), event-driven (after each meeting, generate notes). Match the pattern to the workflow cadence.

concepts.title: Requirements and Limitations
concepts.body: Scheduled tasks require your desktop to be awake (not sleeping) and Claude Desktop to be open. They will not run if your computer is off or the app is closed. This is a local automation system, not a cloud service. For critical tasks, consider pairing with Dispatch (Module 12) for remote triggering.

prompts.label: Create a Daily Morning Briefing Task
prompts.prompt:
Set up a scheduled task that runs every weekday at 8:00 AM and produces a morning briefing. The task should:
1. Check my email for unread messages (Gmail connector)
2. List today's meetings (Calendar connector)
3. Search for top 3 industry news headlines
4. Save everything to outputs/briefing-[date].md
5. Format it as a quick-scan document I can read in 2 minutes

Show me the exact /schedule command and explain each parameter. Also explain what happens if my computer is asleep at 8 AM.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A configured daily scheduled task with clear explanation of the schedule syntax and failure modes.

prompts.label: Schedule Weekly Expense Scanning
prompts.prompt:
Create a scheduled task that runs every Friday at 5 PM and processes my expenses:
1. Scan the references/receipts/ folder for new receipt images and PDFs
2. Extract vendor, date, amount, and category from each receipt
3. Append the data to outputs/expense-tracker.xlsx (create if it does not exist)
4. Generate a weekly summary: total spent, top 3 categories, any receipts that could not be processed
5. Move processed receipts to done/receipts-processed/

Include error handling for receipts that cannot be read. Include a change log.
prompts.expectedOutcome: An automated weekly expense processing pipeline that scans receipts, updates a spreadsheet, and generates a summary.

prompts.label: Manage and Monitor Scheduled Tasks
prompts.prompt:
Help me manage my scheduled tasks:
1. List all currently scheduled tasks with their schedules and last run times
2. Check if any tasks failed on their last run -- show error details
3. Pause a specific task temporarily (without deleting it)
4. Create a monitoring dashboard file: outputs/task-dashboard.md with:
   - Task name, schedule, last run, status (success/failed/paused)
   - Next scheduled run time
   - Any recommended optimizations
5. Explain how to delete a scheduled task I no longer need
prompts.expectedOutcome: A complete view of all scheduled tasks with status monitoring and management instructions.

prompts.label: Build a Scheduled Automation Workflow
prompts.prompt:
In this exercise you will design and prototype a recurring task automation using Claude Code as your planning and scripting partner.

1. Ask Claude to help you design a recurring task workflow for a daily report — describe a realistic scenario (e.g., 'every morning at 8am, check a folder for new CSV files, summarize the data, and append a trend note comparing it to yesterday's output').
2. Ask Claude to generate a cron expression for your chosen schedule and explain what each field means.
3. Ask Claude to write a shell script (or Python script) that simulates one run of the workflow: it should read a sample input file, produce a summary output file, and append a timestamped trend note.
4. Create a sample input file (e.g., sales.csv with a few rows of data), then run the script inside Claude Code and verify the output file is generated correctly.
5. Ask Claude to show you how you would wire this script to a cron job on your system, including the exact crontab entry and any environment variable considerations.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A working automation script that reads a sample CSV, writes a timestamped summary report, and is paired with a ready-to-use crontab entry for recurring scheduled execution.

modules.proTip: Schedule your most important automations for times you are definitely at your desk. If you have a daily standup at 9 AM, schedule your briefing for 8:30 AM so it is ready when you need it.
takeaways: Use /schedule to create daily, weekly, monthly, or custom cron recurring tasks
Desktop must be awake and Claude Desktop must be open for tasks to run
Common patterns: morning briefing, weekly status, monthly expenses, post-meeting notes
Always include error handling in scheduled tasks -- they run unattended

modules.title: Projects and Memory
modules.description: Use Projects for persistent workspaces with their own files, instructions, and memory that survive across sessions.

concepts.title: What Projects Are
concepts.body: Projects are persistent workspaces in Cowork. Each project has its own folder, its own CLAUDE.md, its own files, and its own memory. When you open a project, Cowork loads all of that context automatically. Close the project, open a different one, and the context switches completely. Projects are how you separate work streams without context bleed.

concepts.title: Folder Instructions for Project Context
concepts.body: Each project folder can have its own CLAUDE.md with project-specific instructions. This is where you put: project goals, team members, brand guidelines, preferred tools, output formats, and anything else Cowork needs to know about this specific project. Keep it under 200 lines -- focused context produces better results than exhaustive documentation.

concepts.title: Memory Workarounds for Cross-Session Persistence
concepts.body: Cowork does not have built-in persistent memory across sessions (yet). Workarounds: (1) memories.md file in the project folder -- manually updated with key decisions, people, and context. (2) CLAUDE.md with a 'History' section that captures major milestones. (3) A done/ folder where completed outputs serve as implicit memory. The more structured your memory files, the better Cowork picks up where it left off.

prompts.label: Create a Project Workspace
prompts.prompt:
Set up a new project workspace for a product launch. Create:
1. A project folder called 'product-launch-q2/'
2. CLAUDE.md with: project name, launch date, team members, goals, constraints, and preferred output formats
3. Subfolders: research/, content/, design/, approvals/, outputs/
4. A memories.md with sections: Key Decisions, People & Roles, Brand Notes, Lessons Learned
5. A reference library: references/ with placeholder files for brand-guide.md, competitor-analysis.md, and launch-timeline.md
6. A README.md explaining the project structure

Make all files have real starter content, not just empty headers.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A complete project workspace with structured folders, populated context files, and a reference library.

prompts.label: Set Up Project Memory for Cross-Session Persistence
prompts.prompt:
I want Cowork to remember context across sessions for my active project. Set up a memory system:
1. Create a memories.md with structured sections: People (name, role, preferences), Decisions (date, decision, rationale), Context (ongoing situations, deadlines, dependencies), Lessons (what worked, what did not)
2. Populate it with example entries showing the right format
3. Add instructions to CLAUDE.md telling Cowork to read memories.md at session start and update it at session end
4. Create a prompt I can use at the end of each session: 'Update memories.md with anything new from this session'
5. Explain the limitations and how to handle memory file growth over time
prompts.expectedOutcome: A working cross-session memory system with structured files, update prompts, and growth management guidance.

prompts.label: Build a Reference Library
prompts.prompt:
Create a comprehensive reference library for my project:
1. Audit existing files in my project folder and identify potential reference materials
2. Organize them into references/ with subfolders: brand/, competitors/, templates/, data/
3. Create an index file: references/INDEX.md that catalogs every reference with: title, description, last updated date, and when to use it
4. Add instructions to CLAUDE.md telling Cowork which reference to check for which task type
5. Create 3 starter templates: meeting-notes-template.md, status-update-template.md, decision-log-template.md

The reference library should be a living document that grows with the project.
prompts.expectedOutcome: An organized, indexed reference library with templates and CLAUDE.md integration for automatic reference lookup.

modules.proTip: Keep each project's CLAUDE.md under 200 lines. If it gets longer, split into separate files: goals.md, people.md, conventions.md. Reference them from CLAUDE.md.
takeaways: Projects are persistent workspaces with isolated context -- no bleed between projects
Each project gets its own CLAUDE.md, memories.md, and folder structure
Use memories.md as a cross-session memory bridge -- update it at the end of every session
A structured reference library with an INDEX.md helps Cowork find the right context for each task

modules.title: Sub-agents and Parallel Work
modules.description: Understand how Cowork spawns sub-agents to work in parallel, splitting tasks for dramatic speed improvements.
modules.prerequisites: Module 5 completed (skills). Experience with multi-step Cowork tasks.

concepts.title: How Sub-agents Work
concepts.body: When Cowork faces a large task, it can spawn sub-agents -- separate Claude instances that work on parts of the problem in parallel. Example: processing 10 documents sequentially takes 30 minutes. With sub-agents, 10 parallel workers finish in 4 minutes. Cowork manages the coordination: splitting the work, collecting results, and merging outputs.

concepts.title: The Shared State Problem
concepts.body: Sub-agents do not share memory. Each one gets its own context window and cannot see what the others are doing. This means: no real-time coordination, no shared variables, no "Agent 2, check what Agent 1 found." The orchestrator (main Cowork session) handles merging after all sub-agents complete. Design your tasks to be independently parallelizable.

concepts.title: When to Use Sub-agents Explicitly
concepts.body: Cowork sometimes spawns sub-agents automatically, but you get better results by requesting them explicitly. Use sub-agents when: processing multiple independent files, researching multiple topics, generating multiple variants of content, reviewing from multiple perspectives. Do not use them for tasks that require sequential reasoning or shared context.

prompts.label: Process Multiple Files in Parallel
prompts.prompt:
I have 5 client briefing documents in my references/ folder. Use sub-agents to process them in parallel:
1. Spawn a sub-agent for each document
2. Each sub-agent extracts: client name, key requirements, budget range, timeline, decision-makers
3. Each sub-agent saves its output as a structured summary in outputs/
4. After all sub-agents complete, merge all summaries into a single comparison matrix
5. Output: outputs/client-comparison.md with a table comparing all 5 clients across the extracted dimensions

Explicitly request parallel processing and explain the time savings.
prompts.expectedOutcome: Five parallel document analyses merged into a single comparison matrix, with visible time savings over sequential processing.

prompts.label: Multi-Perspective Research with Sub-agents
prompts.prompt:
I need to research whether to adopt a new project management tool. Use sub-agents to research from 3 perspectives in parallel:

Sub-agent 1 (Feature Analyst): Compare features of Monday.com, Asana, and Linear. Create a feature matrix.
Sub-agent 2 (Cost Analyst): Compare pricing, hidden costs, and ROI for a team of 15. Create a cost table.
Sub-agent 3 (User Analyst): Search for user reviews, complaints, and satisfaction scores. Create a sentiment summary.

After all complete, synthesize the three reports into a single recommendation document with a clear verdict.
prompts.expectedOutcome: Three parallel research streams synthesized into a comprehensive tool comparison with a data-driven recommendation.

prompts.label: Explicit Sub-agent Delegation
prompts.prompt:
I need to prepare for a board meeting next week. Use sub-agents to tackle these tasks in parallel:

Sub-agent 1: Create a financial summary from references/q1-financials.xlsx -- key metrics, trends, and variance analysis
Sub-agent 2: Create a competitive landscape update using web search -- top 3 competitor moves this quarter
Sub-agent 3: Draft talking points for 5 agenda items (I will provide the agenda)
Sub-agent 4: Create a risk register -- top 5 risks with likelihood, impact, and mitigation

Coordination: After all complete, merge into a single board-prep.md with sections for each sub-agent's output. Include a 1-page executive summary at the top.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A comprehensive board meeting prep package assembled from 4 parallel workstreams, with executive summary.

modules.proTip: Sub-agents work best on embarrassingly parallel tasks -- where each piece can be done independently. If tasks need to reference each other's output, run them sequentially instead.
takeaways: Sub-agents are parallel Claude instances that can cut task time from 30 minutes to 4 minutes
Sub-agents do not share memory -- design tasks to be independently parallelizable
Explicitly request sub-agents for multi-file, multi-topic, or multi-perspective tasks
The orchestrator merges results after all sub-agents complete -- plan your merge strategy upfront

modules.title: Computer Use
modules.description: Control desktop applications directly through screen interaction when APIs and connectors are not available.
modules.prerequisites: Module 6 completed (connectors). Understanding of when connectors are insufficient.

concepts.title: Three Modes of Interaction
concepts.body: Cowork interacts with your tools in three ways: (1) Connector-First -- API-level access via built-in connectors (fastest, most reliable). (2) Computer Use -- screen-level control where Cowork sees your screen and clicks/types like a human (slower, handles any app). (3) Dispatch -- remote task assignment from mobile (covered in Module 12). Always prefer connectors. Use Computer Use only when no connector exists.

concepts.title: How Computer Use Works
concepts.body: Computer Use gives Cowork the ability to see your screen, move the mouse, click buttons, type text, and read on-screen content. It is like screen-sharing with a very fast assistant. Cowork takes screenshots, identifies UI elements, and performs actions. It works with any desktop application -- even proprietary tools with no API.

concepts.title: When to Use Computer Use vs Connectors
concepts.body: Use connectors when: the service has a built-in connector (Gmail, Calendar, Slack, Notion) or a Zapier integration. Use Computer Use when: the app has no API (legacy desktop software), the workflow requires visual interaction (drag-and-drop, visual editors), or you need to interact with a web app that has no connector. Computer Use is slower and less reliable than connectors -- it is the fallback, not the default.

prompts.label: Enable and Test Computer Use
prompts.prompt:
I want to try Computer Use for the first time. Walk me through:
1. How to enable Computer Use in Cowork settings
2. What permissions it needs and what it can/cannot do
3. A simple test: open my web browser, navigate to google.com, search for 'Claude Cowork tutorial', and take a screenshot of the results
4. Explain what happened behind the scenes (how Cowork sees the screen, identifies elements, and acts)
5. List 3 practical tasks where Computer Use shines over connectors
prompts.expectedOutcome: A working Computer Use setup with a completed test task and clear understanding of when to use it.

prompts.label: Automate a Desktop Application Task
prompts.prompt:
Use Computer Use to automate a task in a desktop application:
1. Open my default spreadsheet application (Excel or Google Sheets in browser)
2. Create a new spreadsheet
3. Add a header row: Date, Task, Hours, Status
4. Add 5 sample rows of timesheet data
5. Apply basic formatting: bold headers, alternating row colors
6. Save the file to my outputs/ folder

Document each step Cowork takes (screenshot -> identify element -> click/type -> verify). Explain where this is slower or less reliable than using the xlsx skill directly.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A completed spreadsheet created via screen interaction, with documentation of each step and comparison to API-based approaches.

prompts.label: Compare Connector vs Computer Use Approaches
prompts.prompt:
For the same task (checking my calendar and listing today's meetings), run it two ways:

Approach 1 (Connector): Use the Calendar connector to pull today's events via API. Time it.
Approach 2 (Computer Use): Open Google Calendar in a browser, read the screen, and extract today's events visually. Time it.

Compare:
- Speed (which was faster?)
- Accuracy (did both produce the same results?)
- Reliability (which would you trust for a scheduled daily task?)
- Output quality (formatting, completeness)

Summarize with a clear recommendation for when to use each approach.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A side-by-side comparison of connector vs Computer Use approaches with measured performance data and a decision framework.

modules.proTip: Computer Use works on both macOS and Windows (as of April 2026). But always check for a connector first -- API calls are 10x faster and more reliable than screen interactions.
takeaways: Three interaction modes: Connector-First (API), Computer Use (screen), Dispatch (remote)
Always prefer connectors over Computer Use -- faster, more reliable, and more predictable
Computer Use is the fallback for apps without APIs or connectors
Computer Use works by taking screenshots, identifying UI elements, and performing mouse/keyboard actions

modules.title: Dispatch and Remote Control
modules.description: Assign tasks from your phone to your desktop -- fire-and-forget workflows with Dispatch.
modules.prerequisites: Module 8 completed (scheduled tasks). Pro or Max plan subscription.

concepts.title: What Dispatch Is
concepts.body: Dispatch lets you send tasks from your phone to your desktop Cowork session. You type a prompt on mobile, it executes on your desktop where all your files, connectors, and skills live, and the results are delivered back to your phone. It is remote control for your desktop AI assistant.

concepts.title: QR Code Pairing
concepts.body: Dispatch uses QR code pairing to link your phone to your desktop. Open Claude Desktop, go to Dispatch settings, scan the QR code with your phone's camera, and the link is established. Once paired, you can send tasks from anywhere -- on the train, in a meeting, at a coffee shop.

concepts.title: Fire-and-Forget Workflows
concepts.body: The power of Dispatch is fire-and-forget: assign a complex task from your phone, put the phone away, and check the results later. Examples: 'Process the receipts in my inbox and update the expense tracker' -- sent while walking to lunch. 'Generate the weekly report from this week's outputs' -- sent from a taxi. The desktop handles execution, the phone handles assignment.

prompts.label: Set Up Dispatch and Pair Your Phone
prompts.prompt:
Walk me through setting up Dispatch:
1. Where to find Dispatch settings in Claude Desktop
2. How to generate the QR code for pairing
3. How to scan and pair from my phone
4. A test task: from my phone, send 'Create a file called dispatch-test.md with today's date and the text: Dispatch is working!'
5. Verify the file was created on my desktop
6. Explain the requirements (Pro/Max plan, desktop must be on, app must be open)
prompts.expectedOutcome: A working Dispatch connection between phone and desktop, with a verified test task.

prompts.label: Assign a Task from Mobile
prompts.prompt:
I am away from my desk and need to prepare for a meeting in 2 hours. From my phone via Dispatch, assign these tasks:
1. Check my calendar for the meeting details (attendees, agenda)
2. Search my email for any recent threads with the attendees
3. Create a meeting prep document with: attendee profiles, key topics from email threads, 3 questions I should ask, and talking points
4. Save it as outputs/meeting-prep-[date].md

Explain how I would send this as a single Dispatch prompt and how to check the results from my phone.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A meeting prep document generated remotely via Dispatch, with guidance on monitoring task progress from mobile.

prompts.label: Build a Remote Workflow Library
prompts.prompt:
Create a library of 10 Dispatch-optimized prompts I can fire from my phone. Each prompt should:
1. Be short enough to type on a phone (under 50 words)
2. Produce a useful deliverable on my desktop
3. Work without any follow-up interaction (true fire-and-forget)

Categories: Morning routine (3), Meeting prep (2), Client work (2), Expense tracking (1), Content creation (1), End-of-day (1)

Save as references/dispatch-prompts.md with the prompt text and expected output for each.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A curated library of 10 phone-friendly Dispatch prompts for common knowledge worker tasks.

modules.proTip: Write Dispatch prompts like text messages -- short, clear, and action-oriented. Your desktop Cowork session has all the context (CLAUDE.md, files, connectors), so you do not need to repeat it from your phone.
takeaways: Dispatch sends tasks from your phone to your desktop Cowork session
QR code pairing links phone and desktop -- one-time setup
Fire-and-forget: assign complex tasks from anywhere, check results later
Requires Pro or Max plan, desktop must be on with Claude Desktop open

modules.title: Advanced Skill Engineering
modules.description: Apply Anthropic's official skill building patterns to create production-grade skills across three categories: document creation, workflow automation, and MCP enhancement.
modules.prerequisites: Module 5 completed (skills basics). Module 7 completed (plugins). Real-world skill building experience.

concepts.title: Three Skill Categories
concepts.body: Anthropic's official guide identifies three categories: (1) Document/Asset Creation -- skills that produce files (reports, presentations, spreadsheets, designs). (2) Workflow Automation -- skills that orchestrate multi-step processes (email triage, expense processing, content pipelines). (3) MCP Enhancement -- skills that extend Cowork's capabilities by wrapping MCP server tools with better instructions and error handling.

concepts.title: Success Criteria and Testing
concepts.body: Every skill needs measurable success criteria. For document skills: does the output match the spec? For workflow skills: does every step complete without manual intervention? For MCP skills: does the enhancement improve reliability over raw tool calls? Test skills with edge cases: missing inputs, unexpected data formats, API failures. A skill that fails silently is worse than no skill at all.

concepts.title: Distribution and Sharing
concepts.body: Share skills with your team via GitHub, the Plugin Marketplace, or direct file sharing. When distributing: include a README with usage examples, document all dependencies (connectors, MCP servers, file structure), version your skills (semver), and include test prompts so recipients can verify the skill works in their environment.

prompts.label: Build a Category 1 Skill: Document Creation
prompts.prompt:
Build a production-grade document creation skill: a 'Proposal Generator' that takes a client name, project scope, and budget range, then produces a polished PDF proposal. The SKILL.md should include:
1. Input validation (reject missing fields with clear error messages)
2. Template structure (cover page, executive summary, scope, timeline, pricing, terms)
3. Dynamic content population from inputs and reference files
4. Professional formatting with consistent branding
5. Output verification (check the PDF has all required sections)
6. Error handling (what happens if the PDF skill fails?)

Include success criteria and 3 test cases with expected outputs.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A production-grade proposal generator skill with input validation, error handling, and test cases.

prompts.label: Build a Category 3 Skill: MCP Enhancement
prompts.prompt:
Build an MCP Enhancement skill that wraps the Gmail connector with intelligent email triage. The skill should:
1. Pull unread emails from the last 24 hours (Gmail connector)
2. Classify each email: Urgent, Action Required, FYI, Newsletter, Spam
3. For Urgent emails: draft a quick response and flag for review
4. For Action Required: extract the action item and add to todo/
5. For Newsletters: extract key points and add to a daily digest
6. Handle failures gracefully (Gmail API timeout, classification uncertainty)

The skill should be a wrapper that makes raw Gmail connector calls smarter, more reliable, and more useful. Include the SKILL.md with full error handling.
prompts.expectedOutcome: An MCP Enhancement skill that transforms raw Gmail API calls into intelligent email triage with classification, action extraction, and draft responses.

prompts.label: Test and Iterate a Skill
prompts.prompt:
Take one of my existing custom skills and put it through a rigorous testing and iteration cycle:
1. Run the skill with normal inputs -- document the output quality (1-10)
2. Run with edge cases: empty inputs, very long inputs, malformed data
3. Run with failure conditions: missing reference files, disconnected connectors
4. For each failure, add error handling to the SKILL.md
5. Re-run all tests and compare quality scores
6. Create a test-results.md documenting: test cases, expected vs actual outputs, pass/fail, and improvements made

Target: the skill should handle all edge cases gracefully without crashing or producing garbage output.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A battle-tested skill with documented test results, improved error handling, and verified edge case coverage.

modules.proTip: The best skills are ones you would be embarrassed to share if they failed. Build with the assumption that someone else will use your skill with unexpected inputs.
takeaways: Three categories: Document/Asset Creation, Workflow Automation, MCP Enhancement
Every skill needs measurable success criteria and test cases
Test with edge cases, missing inputs, and failure conditions before distributing
Include README, version, dependencies, and test prompts when sharing skills

modules.title: Power User Workflows
modules.description: Build real-world production workflows -- business OS dashboards, content pipelines, research synthesis, contract review, and email triage automation.
modules.prerequisites: Modules 1-13 completed. Multiple connectors configured. Custom skills built.

concepts.title: The Business OS Concept
concepts.body: A Business OS is a visual map of your entire Cowork system: all skills, connectors, scheduled tasks, and data flows in one view. It shows how your automation pieces connect: Gmail feeds into email triage, which feeds into task management, which feeds into weekly reports. Building this map reveals gaps, redundancies, and optimization opportunities.

concepts.title: Content Repurposing Pipelines
concepts.body: Take one piece of content and automatically produce 5-10 variants for different platforms. A blog post becomes a LinkedIn post, a Twitter thread, an email newsletter section, a slide deck summary, and a podcast script. Each step is a skill. The pipeline is a scheduled task or a single prompt that chains them all.

concepts.title: Research Synthesis Across Multiple Sources
concepts.body: Cowork can synthesize information from multiple sources in a single workflow: web search results, email threads, uploaded documents, Notion databases, and Slack channels. The key is structuring the synthesis: define the question, identify sources, extract relevant information from each, cross-reference, and produce a unified analysis with source attribution.

concepts.title: Contract Review and Analysis
concepts.body: Upload a contract and Cowork will: extract key terms (parties, dates, obligations, payment terms), flag unusual clauses, compare against a template, highlight risks, and produce a summary brief. This is not legal advice -- it is document analysis that saves hours of reading. Always have a lawyer review the final output.

prompts.label: Build a Business OS Dashboard
prompts.prompt:
Create a visual map of my entire Cowork automation system. Produce a file called outputs/business-os.md that includes:
1. Inventory: List all my installed skills, active connectors, and scheduled tasks
2. Data Flow Map: Show how data flows between components (email -> triage -> tasks -> reports)
3. Gap Analysis: Identify workflows I do manually that could be automated
4. Optimization Opportunities: Where am I duplicating effort or wasting credits?
5. Recommended Additions: Top 5 skills, connectors, or tasks I should add
6. System Health Score: Rate my setup out of 10 with specific improvement actions

Format as a structured dashboard I can review weekly.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A comprehensive system map revealing the full automation landscape, gaps, and optimization opportunities.

prompts.label: Build a Content Repurposing Pipeline
prompts.prompt:
I have a blog post in references/latest-blog.md. Build a content repurposing pipeline that produces:
1. A LinkedIn post (professional tone, 150-200 words, with a hook and CTA)
2. A Twitter/X thread (5-7 tweets, punchy, with numbered format)
3. An email newsletter section (3 paragraphs, conversational tone)
4. A presentation slide outline (5 slides with titles and bullet points)
5. A podcast script talking points (2-minute segment, casual tone)

Save each as a separate file in outputs/content-repurpose/. Include a manifest listing all outputs and their word counts.

Use sub-agents to generate all 5 variants in parallel.
prompts.expectedOutcome: Five platform-specific content variants generated in parallel from a single source blog post.

prompts.label: Automate Email Triage
prompts.prompt:
Build a complete email triage automation:
1. Connect to Gmail and pull unread emails from the last 12 hours
2. Classify each email: Urgent (needs response today), Action (needs response this week), FYI (informational), Newsletter (content to digest), Noise (can be archived)
3. For Urgent: draft a response and save to outputs/email-drafts/
4. For Action: extract the action item and add to todo/email-actions.md
5. For Newsletter: extract the top insight and add to a running digest at outputs/newsletter-digest.md
6. For Noise: list them in outputs/archive-candidates.md
7. Create a summary: outputs/email-triage-[date].md with counts per category and time estimate to clear the inbox

This should be schedulable as a daily automated task.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A full email triage system with classification, draft responses, action extraction, and a daily summary report.

prompts.label: Research Synthesis from Multiple Sources
prompts.prompt:
I need to research 'AI adoption in mid-market B2B companies' using multiple sources. Build a synthesis workflow:
1. Web search: Find 5 recent articles and reports on the topic
2. Extract key findings from each source with citations
3. Cross-reference findings: which claims appear in multiple sources? Which are unique?
4. Identify contradictions or disagreements between sources
5. Produce a unified research brief:
   - Executive summary (200 words)
   - Key findings with confidence levels (High: 3+ sources, Medium: 2 sources, Low: 1 source)
   - Data points and statistics table
   - Gaps in the research (what could not be answered)
   - Source bibliography

Save as outputs/research-ai-adoption.md
prompts.expectedOutcome: A rigorous multi-source research brief with confidence scoring, cross-referenced findings, and identified gaps.

modules.proTip: Build your Business OS incrementally: start with one automated workflow that saves 30 minutes per week. Once it is reliable, add the next one. A fragile system of 20 automations is worse than 3 rock-solid ones.
takeaways: A Business OS maps your entire automation system and reveals gaps and redundancies
Content repurposing pipelines turn one piece into 5-10 platform-specific variants
Research synthesis with confidence scoring turns multiple sources into actionable intelligence
Email triage automation with classification and draft responses saves hours weekly

modules.title: Safety, Limits, and Optimization
modules.description: Manage credits, understand safety boundaries, troubleshoot common issues, and optimize your Cowork setup for maximum efficiency.
modules.prerequisites: Modules 1-14 completed. Active Cowork usage for at least 2 weeks.

concepts.title: Credit Management and Usage Tracking
concepts.body: Cowork consumes credits based on token usage. Every prompt, every sub-agent, every file read costs tokens. Track your usage in Settings > Usage. Optimize by: writing shorter, more specific prompts. Using skills (loaded once) instead of repeating instructions. Avoiding unnecessary file reads. Compacting context regularly. The goal is maximum output per credit spent.

concepts.title: Safety Checklist
concepts.body: Cowork runs in a VM sandbox with restricted permissions. Safety boundaries: it cannot access system files outside your work folder, it cannot install software, it cannot make network calls except through configured connectors. File deletion requires explicit permission. Always review the change log after tasks. Never give Cowork access to folders containing passwords, keys, or sensitive credentials.

concepts.title: Troubleshooting Common Issues
concepts.body: Session ends on app close: Cowork sessions do not survive closing Claude Desktop. Save progress frequently. Usage limits: Pro plan has usage caps that reset monthly. File access issues: Cowork can only access your designated work folder. Connector failures: tokens expire, re-authenticate in Settings. Slow performance: too many connectors loaded, too much context, or complex Computer Use tasks.

concepts.title: Cowork vs Competitors
concepts.body: Cowork vs Gemini Deep Research: Cowork produces files and takes actions, Gemini produces research reports. Cowork vs Copilot: Cowork has deeper file access and skill system, Copilot has tighter Microsoft Office integration. Cowork's unique strengths: skills system, plugin ecosystem, sub-agents, Dispatch, and the connector library. Choose based on your ecosystem and workflow needs.

prompts.label: Audit Your Safety Settings
prompts.prompt:
Run a safety audit of my Cowork configuration:
1. Check what folders Cowork has access to -- are any sensitive folders exposed?
2. Review installed connectors -- are any granted more permissions than needed?
3. Check if any scheduled tasks could cause data loss (file deletion, overwriting)
4. Verify that CLAUDE.md does not contain any credentials, API keys, or passwords
5. Review Computer Use permissions -- is screen access appropriately restricted?
6. Create a safety checklist file: outputs/safety-audit.md with pass/fail for each check

Score my safety posture out of 10 and recommend fixes for anything below 8.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A comprehensive safety audit with scored checklist and specific recommendations for improving security posture.

prompts.label: Optimize Credit Usage
prompts.prompt:
Help me optimize my Cowork credit usage:
1. Review my recent task history -- which tasks consumed the most credits?
2. Identify patterns: am I re-reading the same files repeatedly? Are my prompts too verbose?
3. Suggest 5 specific changes to reduce credit consumption by 30%+
4. Create an optimized version of my 3 most-used prompts (same output, fewer tokens)
5. Recommend which tasks should use skills (one-time load) vs fresh prompts (per-invocation cost)
6. Calculate estimated monthly credit savings from the optimizations

Save the optimization plan as outputs/credit-optimization.md
prompts.expectedOutcome: A credit optimization plan with specific prompt rewrites, usage pattern analysis, and estimated monthly savings.

prompts.label: Build a Safety and Troubleshooting Guide
prompts.prompt:
Create a comprehensive troubleshooting guide for my Cowork setup:
1. Common issues and fixes: session loss, connector failures, skill not loading, file access errors, Computer Use failures
2. Safety best practices: folder access, credential management, deletion protection, change log review
3. Performance optimization: context management, connector pruning, prompt efficiency
4. Emergency procedures: what to do if Cowork modifies wrong files, how to undo changes, how to revoke connector access
5. Weekly maintenance checklist: update skills, review scheduled tasks, audit connectors, check credit usage

Save as references/troubleshooting-guide.md -- a living document I can update over time.
prompts.expectedOutcome: A comprehensive troubleshooting and maintenance guide covering safety, performance, and emergency procedures.

modules.proTip: The #1 credit saver is specificity. A 50-word prompt that produces the right output on the first try costs far less than a 200-word prompt that needs 3 rounds of revision.
takeaways: Track credit usage in Settings > Usage and optimize prompts for token efficiency
Cowork runs in a VM sandbox -- it cannot access system files or install software
Never expose folders with passwords, API keys, or sensitive credentials to Cowork
Weekly maintenance: update skills, audit connectors, review scheduled tasks, check safety

capstones.title: White Belt Challenge
capstones.description: Set up a complete Cowork workspace from scratch -- proving you can configure the environment for productive autonomous work.
tasks: Create a dedicated work folder with the recommended 6-folder structure
Write Global Instructions that capture your role, preferences, and work style
Create CLAUDE.md, branding.md, and memories.md with real, useful content
Delegate 3 tasks using the three-question framework and review the change logs

capstones.title: Brown Belt Challenge
capstones.description: Build a full automated workflow -- custom skill + connectors + scheduled task that runs daily without manual intervention.
tasks: Build a custom skill that produces a daily deliverable (briefing, report, or digest)
Connect at least 2 connectors (Gmail + Calendar, Slack + Notion, etc.)
Create a scheduled task that runs the skill daily at a fixed time
Verify it runs 5 consecutive days without manual intervention

capstones.title: Black Belt Challenge
capstones.description: Build a Business OS plugin -- a complete plugin with 5+ skills, connector integrations, scheduled tasks, and a visual dashboard of your entire system.
tasks: Build a plugin with 5+ skills covering your core workflows
Integrate at least 3 connectors into the plugin
Include at least 2 scheduled tasks (daily and weekly)
Create a Business OS dashboard showing all automations, data flows, and system health
Document the plugin with README, test prompts, and distribution instructions

bonusPrompts.label: Weekly Report Generator from Daily Outputs

citations: Jack Roberts, "Claude Cowork Full Course" (YouTube, 1:05:18, 77K views)
Brock Mesarich, "15 Claude Skills Tutorial" (YouTube, 88K views)
Karo Zieminski, "50+ Claude Power User Tips"
Anthropic, "Official Skill Building Guide" (32 pages, PDF)

categories: Category 1: Document/Asset Creation -- skills that produce files (reports, presentations, spreadsheets)
Category 2: Workflow Automation -- skills that orchestrate multi-step processes
Category 3: MCP Enhancement -- skills that wrap connector tools with better instructions

threeQuestions: What does 'done' look like? (exact deliverable description)
What context does Claude need? (reference files, examples, brand guides)
What constraints can it not guess? (deadlines, audience, tone, exclusions)
